behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the model’s content decision quality: whether the draft actually grounds itself in thread-specific details. A mock/stub model would hide this failure by returning canned grounded text. I considered Workflow but rejected it because the behavior text targets one model-output property (grounded reply content), not correctness across multiple agentic handoffs.
behaviors.test_description: Run generation with a real LLM on real sampled email threads and assert the reply contains concrete thread-specific references (entities, requests, dates, or commitments) instead of generic phrasing; repeat across runs/prompts to expose non-deterministic misses.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily a module-boundary behavior: draft-claim extraction/normalization plus KB lookup and contradiction logic. Real LLM output is not required if the test injects deterministic draft claims. Integration-brake checks: no required OS/network/subprocess boundary, no explicit AI/ML judgment requirement in behavior text, and no multi-agentic chain. I considered Agentic but rejected it because the behavior text says only 'flags claims ... against the knowledge base' and does not require model-quality evaluation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the factual-check service with a real local KB adapter (or local test DB) and feed crafted draft statements that contradict KB facts; assert contradiction flags include the specific offending claims and KB evidence references.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Could be Unit if implemented as one pure function, but minimum robust boundary is usually claim parsing + KB access wiring, so Integration is consistent.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: Even with deterministic 'no KB results' input, correctness depends on what the real LLM chooses to write (disclaimer vs invented facts). Mocking the model can always pass and hide hallucination failures. I considered Integration but rejected it because the behavior asserts output-content quality under uncertainty ('rather than fabricating facts'), which is model judgment.
behaviors.test_description: Execute drafting with real LLM and empty/irrelevant KB retrieval results, then assert the draft includes the required uncertainty disclaimer and does not introduce unsupported factual claims; run multiple trials to catch variability.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at orchestration/state-handoff boundaries (draft state, approval state, send trigger), not model quality. A multi-module in-process test with a fake sender can catch premature send calls. Integration-brake checks: no required real OS/network/subprocess behavior, no AI/ML-output dependence, no multi-agentic chain. I considered System but rejected it because the behavior text is a gating rule ('blocks sending until user explicitly approves') that can be validated without real external mail transport.
behaviors.test_description: Test approval controller + send service wiring: create draft, do not approve, verify no send invocation; then submit explicit approval event and verify exactly one send invocation with the approved draft.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets the full agent pipeline across sequential stages, including at least one real LLM generation step and downstream handoffs. Real end-to-end failures often arise only across stage boundaries (thread ingestion -> generation -> fact check -> approval presentation). I considered Agentic but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly requires the 'full sequence' end-to-end, i.e., pipeline-level correctness rather than a single model decision.
behaviors.test_description: Run an end-to-end scenario on a real email thread source: ingest thread, generate via real LLM, run factual check against KB, surface approval UI/state, and verify artifacts and transitions at each stage with final send still blocked pending approval.
